text: The surgical team drapes the patient widely to maximize space on the abdominal wall for port placement, ensuring adequate room to prevent collisions between the robotic arms.
The surgeon inserts the camera port through the umbilicus to initiate the robotic cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon inserts an optical blunt tip trocar into the abdominal cavity under direct visualization using a laparoscope.

The surgeon places the first port in the very lateral right upper quadrant, entering in a horizontal plane above the colon in the right gutter. This lateral placement maximizes space to avoid collisions between robotic arms.

The surgeon places the next port site in the midclavicular line of the right upper quadrant, positioned halfway between the umbilical camera port and the lateral port number three. This port is situated more superiorly compared to the lateral port.

The surgeon places the left upper quadrant port with a side port for smoke irrigation, positioning it more laterally and inferiorly than in a standard four-port laparoscopic cholecystectomy. This placement avoids interference from the falciform ligament during instrument insertion.

The surgical team positions the patient with the table in its lowest position, angled fifteen to twenty degrees in reverse Trendelenburg and slightly turned to the left. The robotic system is docked over the patient's right shoulder, aligning the camera port, gallbladder, and robotic column in a straight line for optimal access.

The camera arm is attached to the trocar at the umbilicus. The left upper quadrant arm is then attached to its port site, followed by the attachment of arm number three in the lateral right upper quadrant, and finally the midclavicular right upper quadrant arm. Either the midclavicular or lateral right upper quadrant port can be left undocked for assistance.
The robotic arms are positioned in the final configuration, spread out significantly from one another to minimize the risk of collisions during the procedure.
The surgeon enters the abdomen and inspects the port sites to ensure the remote centers are correctly positioned within the muscle. Instruments are then inserted under direct visualization.

The surgeon begins dissection by lysing adhesions between the omentum and the gallbladder, which are extensive due to previous attacks. The adhesions are carefully separated from the intra-abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar device with arm number two to grasp and retract adhesions on the left side of the screen, aiming to clear the area and identify the gallbladder anatomy. The device is chosen for its low grip strength, minimizing the risk of injury to the friable tissues affected by acute inflammation.
On the right side of the screen, the surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel in arm number one to divide adhesions, while the fenestrated bipolar device assists in hemostasis with bipolar cautery.

The surgeon observes the gallbladder, which appears extremely friable and prone to tearing, resulting in bile spillage and tissue oozing. A suction irrigation device is employed to manage the bile and fluid accumulation.

The surgeon switches to the Maryland dissector to perform precise dissection around the cystic arteries. The instrument's wrist action and fine tips allow for detailed dissection, while its bipolar cautery function aids in achieving hemostasis.

The surgeon works high on the gallbladder to avoid injury to structures in Calot's triangle. A branch of the cystic artery is identified and clipped using Hemlock clips, followed by division of the artery branch with the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to maintain hemostasis and a clear visual field while manipulating the friable and inflamed gallbladder tissues. The device aids in blunt dissection, helping to define the anatomy and manage bleeding.

The surgeon continues to clear adhesions from the anterior surface of the gallbladder, progressing toward its base. Despite the distorted anatomy due to acute inflammation, Hartmann's pouch becomes visible as the dissection advances.

The surgeon uses the suction irrigator for blunt dissection at the base of the gallbladder, identifying a second branch of the cystic artery. Hemlock clips are applied to divide the artery, maintaining hemostasis while avoiding injury to structures at the gallbladder base.

The surgeon continues to work inferiorly, using a harmonic scalpel to dissect the mid portion of the gallbladder free from the liver bed, while leaving the top adherent for retraction. A fan retractor is introduced through the lateral right upper quadrant to retract the omentum and transverse colon, improving visualization. A five millimeter port is added for additional retraction by the assistant. With enhanced visibility, the surgeon dissects fatty tissue and adhesions around Hartman's pouch, distinguishing them from biliary tree structures.

The surgeon utilizes HD-Vision to identify important structures and avoid injury during the robotic cholecystectomy. After completing the posterior wall dissection of the gallbladder, the cystic duct is clearly identified and skeletonized from surrounding scar tissue. Clips are applied to the cystic duct, which is then divided and removed from the base of the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses monopolar cautery scissors to dissect the remaining portion of the gallbladder from the liver bed, working superiorly along the posterior wall. Hemostasis is maintained with cautery, and a portion of the gallbladder is left attached to the liver for retraction. Upon inspecting the liver bed, a small posterior branch is identified as bleeding, and a hemostatic clip is applied.

The robotic platform provides enhanced visualization, allowing the surgeon to identify small vessels and apply clips or bipolar cautery for hemostasis. The suction irrigator is used to aspirate blood and fluids, ensuring thorough inspection for bleeding. A starch-based Rista Coagulant is applied to the friable liver bed to prevent postoperative bleeding. The procedure concludes with the division of the remaining attachments of the gallbladder to the liver.
